one_line_takeaway: PUPPET uses DPO with a combined detector+task-metric reward to fine-tune an LLM so its outputs are simultaneously more detectable as machine-generated and better on downstream task metrics than five watermarking baselines, and the detectability signal proves far more robust to paraphrasing attacks (-1.2 AUROC) than watermarking (-28.5 to -49.2 AUROC).
key_finding: On Llama-3 (Table 1, in-domain), PUPPET reaches AUROC 100.0/99.8/99.3 on ELI5/Multi-News/IELTS respectively (vs. Vanilla 97.1/92.8/87.0), while simultaneously raising ROUGE-L from 22.9->25.8 (ELI5) and 26.5->28.0 (Multi-News), and raising the LLM-as-a-Judge IELTS score from 6.77->7.19 -- all statistically significant task-performance gains (paired t-test, p<0.05). Against the five watermarking baselines, PUPPET matches or exceeds their AUROC on 2 of 3 benchmarks relative to the strongest baseline (KGW) while beating all of them on task performance, e.g. on IELTS PUPPET's judge score of 7.19 exceeds the best watermark baseline SynthID's 6.76. Generalization: a model trained only on ELI5 still raises Multi-News AUROC from 92.8->97.3 and IELTS AUROC from 87.0->94.9 (Table 2), i.e. detectability gains transfer out-of-domain. Robustness: gains hold on Qwen3-4B/8B/14B (e.g. Qwen3-8B average detection 81.2->94.1, task 44.4->46.0, Table 3) and on two additional detectors, FakeSpotAI and MAGE (Table 4). Under Dipper paraphrasing attack, all five watermarking baselines lose 28.5-49.2 AUROC points on average, whereas PUPPET's AUROC drops by only 1.2 points (Figure 3). Data efficiency: on ELI5, AUROC already reaches 100.0 at only 1,000 training samples (Figure 4), and each run takes ~1-2 GPU hours on a single A6000, contrasted by the authors against OLMo3's 150K-200K DPO pairs. Reward ablation (Figure 5) shows the detector-only reward (S_Det, alpha=1) gains up to +12.9 AUROC but degrades task performance by up to -1.5 ROUGE-L/-0.3 Judge, while the evaluator-only reward (S_Eval, alpha=0) gains up to +3.1 ROUGE-L/+0.8 Judge but gives no reliable detection gain -- PUPPET's combined reward recovers 95.3-100% of the detector-only detection gain while still improving task performance, showing both reward terms are needed. Model-attribution analysis (Table 5) shows PUPPET widens the detector-likelihood gap between models (Cohen's d up to 4.62 on IELTS), raising attribution AUROC to 94.47-99.14 vs. 58.99-77.71 for two Vanilla models, though attribution collapses back toward chance (54.54-87.22) when both compared models are themselves PUPPET-trained.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is PUPPET, a DPO-based fine-tuning framework that trains an LLM to produce text that is simultaneously more detectable as machine-generated AND better on downstream task metrics, rather than trading one off against the other as watermarking does. For each query, k=5 candidate responses are sampled from the base model, each is scored by (1) a detector score S_Det = p(Machine | text) from a machine-text detector and (2) an evaluator score S_Eval = a task-specific metric (ROUGE-L or LLM-as-a-Judge); the two z-normalized scores are combined into S = alpha*z(S_Det) + (1-alpha)*z(S_Eval), and the max/min-scoring candidates become the 'chosen'/'rejected' pair for DPO training (with LoRA) of the base model. This is presented as an alternative to logit/sampling-based watermarking (KGW, SynthID, Unigram, EXPGumbel, K-SemStamp), which the authors note optimizes only detectability and can degrade task performance in prior literature. An exploratory extension shows the learned detectability features can also support model attribution (telling PUPPET-trained model outputs apart from other models' outputs).
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-attacker-vs-defender security paper in the classic sense; it targets an AI-accountability/provenance problem: an LLM provider wants its own outputs to be reliably identifiable as machine-generated (for misuse tracking/accountability) without sacrificing usefulness, which would otherwise push users toward less-detectable, less-accountable alternatives. The one adversarial element evaluated is evasion: the paper tests robustness of PUPPET's detectability signal against a paraphrasing attack (Dipper, with lex/order diversity=60, following Krishna et al. 2023) as a stand-in for an adversary trying to strip the detectable signal from generated text. There is no adversary trying to fool the task-performance evaluator, and no threat model around detector poisoning, defeating attribution, or generating text that impersonates another model.
limitations: The paper itself notes: (1) the reported task-performance degradation of watermarking from prior work was not consistently reproduced in their own experiments, which they conjecture may be because modern LLMs stay fluent under distributional constraints, but they explicitly state they 'do not conduct a dedicated analysis to substantiate this claim'; (2) the model-attribution results are called 'exploratory'/'preliminary evidence,' not a validated capability; (3) only a single fixed hyperparameter configuration (alpha=0.5, k=5) was used without systematic hyperparameter search, left as future work; (4) Llama-3-70B-Instruct was excluded from model-size robustness checks due to compute constraints, so size robustness is only shown on the smaller Qwen3 family (4B/8B/14B); (5) the Ethics Statement flags that detection approaches (including PUPPET and watermarking) remain research-stage and should be used cautiously where false positives (flagging human text as machine-generated) could cause harm; (6) evaluation uses only ROUGE-L and one LLM-as-a-judge metric as task-performance proxies, and detectability is evaluated against a limited set of detectors/attacks (3 detectors, one paraphrasing attack family).

datasets_used: ELI5 (long-form QA; via WaterBench, sourced from Hello-SimpleAI/HC3 reddit_eli5 for training)
Multi-News (summarization; via WaterBench, sourced from alexfabbri/multi_news for training)
quantitative_results: Llama-3 in-domain (Table 1): PUPPET AUROC 100.0/99.8/99.3 on ELI5/Multi-News/IELTS vs Vanilla 97.1/92.8/87.0
Llama-3 in-domain task performance: ROUGE-L 25.8 (ELI5) and 28.0 (Multi-News) for PUPPET vs 22.9/26.5 for Vanilla; IELTS Judge score 7.19 for PUPPET vs 6.77 for Vanilla
PUPPET vs strongest watermark baseline (KGW) on IELTS: Judge 7.19 vs SynthID's 6.76 (task-performance gap up to ~6%)
Out-of-domain transfer (Table 2): ELI5-trained PUPPET raises Multi-News AUROC 92.8->97.3 and IELTS AUROC 87.0->94.9

Qwen3 model-size/family robustness (Table 3): Qwen3-8B avg detection 81.2->94.1, avg task 44.4->46.0; Qwen3-14B avg detection 76.0->97.4, avg task 45.2->47.1; Qwen3-4B avg detection 88.7->97.1, avg task 44.2->45.3
Detector robustness (Table 4): OpenAI detector avg 92.3->99.7 (det.)/41.5->44.5 (task); FakeSpotAI 99.9->100.0/41.5->44.2; MAGE 95.5->96.5/41.5->45.0
Paraphrasing (Dipper) attack (Figure 3): AUROC drop averaged over 3 benchmarks -- KGW -28.5, SynthID -40.9, Unigram -49.2, EXPGumbel -40.6, K-SemStamp -36.3, PUPPET only -1.2
Training efficiency: on ELI5, AUROC reaches 100.0 with only 1,000 training samples; ~1-2 GPU hours per run on a single NVIDIA A6000 (48GB)

Reward ablation (Figure 5, Llama-3): S_Det-only up to +12.9 AUROC but -1.5 ROUGE-L/-0.3 Judge; S_Eval-only up to +3.1 ROUGE-L/+0.8 Judge but negligible AUROC gain; PUPPET recovers 95.3-100% of S_Det-only's detection gain while still improving task metrics

Model attribution AUROC (Table 5, OpenAI Detector): Vanilla Llama-3 vs Vanilla Qwen3 = 58.99-77.71 (Cohen's d 0.23-0.96); PUPPET Llama-3 vs Vanilla Qwen3 = 94.47-99.14 (d up to 4.62); PUPPET Llama-3 vs PUPPET Qwen3 = 54.54-87.22 (d 0.22-1.21)
baselines_compared: Vanilla (unmodified base LLM, no PUPPET fine-tuning, evaluated with OpenAI Detector as lower-bound reference)
